claims.quote: Like WaniKani, Bunpro also asks you to type in the answers by using something known as cloze deletion or a cloze test. You're given example sentences with a fill-in-the-blanks gap. You need to type in the correct grammar point... psychologist John Glover found the cloze deletion form of cued recall more effective for long-term learning than a simple recognition test

claims.claim: Bunpro mitigates review overload from incorrect answers via a configurable 'SRS strictness' setting and a 'ghosts' system, so a wrong answer drops the item part-way (e.g. halves the SRS level and adds a ghost) instead of forcing a full Anki-style reset to stage one.
claims.quote: I went ahead and set it so that my SRS level decreases by half if I get it wrong, and adds a ghost... It has definitely increased my retention while still being less punishing than a complete reset.

claims.claim: A 144-page academic study (Alice Joko, Universidade de Brasília, 2011) performed a contrastive analysis of the Japanese and Portuguese phonological systems explicitly to provide subsidies for teaching Japanese to Brazilian Portuguese speakers, establishing that such an L1-specific contrastive basis exists in the literature.
claims.quote: Análise constrativa dos sistemas fonológicos do japonês e do português: subsídios para o ensino de japonês para falantes do português do Brasil ... Alice Joko ... 2011 ... 144 pages

claims.claim: The study, after the contrastive phonology analysis, compiled an inventory of the principal pronunciation errors of beginning Brazilian learners of Japanese, distinguishing errors of PERCEPTION from errors of REPRODUCTION — i.e., Portuguese L1 learners systematically mis-perceive and mis-produce certain Japanese contrasts.
claims.quote: LEVANTAMENTO DE ERROS Na parte 4 deste trabalho, fizemos um estudo contrastivo dos sistemas fonológicos do japonês e do português e mostramos as semelhanças e diferenças dos mesmos. Nesta parte, faremos o levantamento dos principais erros, quanto à percepção e à reprodução. 5.1 -Erros de Percepção O quadro n° 11 mostra ... as palavras em que os alunos que estão se iniciando no estudo da língua

claims.claim: Pitch accent (acento) is treated as a distinct, dedicated dimension of the Japanese-vs-Portuguese contrast (the study draws on multiple works on Tokyo pitch accent and on Portuguese lexical vs phrasal accent), implying tone/pitch is a separate pedagogical concern beyond segment-level kana sounds for Brazilian learners.
claims.quote: MIYAKE, Takeo. On the Three and Two Levei Analysis of Tokyo Pitch Accent ... MORAES, João Antônio. Acentuação Lexical e Acentuação Frasal em Português. Um Estudo Acústico-Perceptivo

claims.claim: cmi5 is an xAPI 'profile' (a set of extra interoperability rules) specifically designed for the use case where a learner launches learning content/activities from an LMS user interface, and it explicitly defines rules for 'Course Structure' as one of its five governed areas.
claims.quote: cmi5 defines specific interoperability rules for the following areas: Content Launch Mechanism, Authentication, Session Management, Reporting, Course Structure

claims.claim: cmi5 governs course structure but defers the broader tracking concerns to a deliberately minimal data model, defining only the bare-minimum required data elements (score, status, time) rather than a rich prescriptive sequencing/navigation model.
claims.quote: The goal of simple data model is to only define the bare minimum data elements required that would work across most learning domains. (e.g. Score, status, and time).

claims.claim: cmi5 supports a Content-as-a-Service (CaaS) delivery model in which learning content can be hosted on domains independent of the LMS server domain, which is relevant to a Flutter app that references a corpus/content layer separately from its course-index layer.
claims.quote: 3 - Support for content as a service (CaaS) model of delivery / Allow content to be stored on other domains (independent of the LMS server domain)

claims.claim: cmi5 was created by the AICC starting in 2010 as an intended successor to both AICC and SCORM, motivated by those specifications' data models being too complicated with many unused optional data elements.
claims.quote: SCORM and AICC data models were too complicated and had many optional data elements that were not used.

claims.claim: cmi5 includes an extensible content-defined data model plus the ability to share data between learning activities for multiple learners enrolled in the same course, supporting content designers adding features while remaining interoperable.
claims.quote: 5 - Share data between learning activities / Allow data to be shared between learning activities for multiple learners enrolled in the same course.

claims.claim: Anki's desired retention default is 90% as a balance of retention and workload; raising it above 90% increases workload very quickly and above 97% the workload becomes overwhelming. This gives a concrete, citable default and upper bound for an FSRS-backed course's per-deck retention target.
claims.quote: Choose a value of desired retention: the proportion of cards recalled successfully when they are due. This is the most important setting in FSRS. Higher retention leads to shorter intervals and more reviews per day. The default is 90%, which offers a good balance of retention and workload. Above 90% the workload increases very quickly, and above 97% the workload can be overwhelming.

claims.claim: FSRS parameters and desired retention are preset-specific (so different decks can carry different settings), but FSRS itself can only be toggled globally, not per-preset. This directly informs the per-deck-vs-global parameter question for a multi-deck (kana/kanji/vocab/grammar) course.
claims.quote: FSRS can only be enabled globally; you cannot enable it for some presets and disable it for others. ... Parameters and desired retention are preset-specific, so you can create multiple presets with different settings.

claims.claim: Deck options are bundled into reusable presets that multiple decks can share; changing an option in a preset propagates to every deck using it, and all new decks start on the Default preset. This is the canonical model for sharing scheduler config across many decks/topics in a linear course.
claims.quote: Anki allows you to share options between different decks, to make it easier to update options in many decks at once. To do this, options are grouped into presets. If you change an option in a preset, the change is applied to all decks that use the same preset. All newly created decks use the "Default" preset.

claims.claim: The new-cards-per-day introduction rate drives total review load roughly 10x: consistently learning 20 new cards/day yields about 200 reviews/day, and the lever to avoid review overload is reducing the number of new cards introduced per day. This is concrete guidance for capping new-card unlock rate in a linear course to prevent review pile-up.
claims.quote: If you are consistently learning 20 new cards a day, you can expect your daily reviews to be roughly about 200 cards/day. You can decrease the reviews required by introducing fewer new cards each day until your review burden decreases.

claims.claim: The New/Review Order setting controls whether new cards are mixed in with reviews, shown before them, or shown after them (i.e., interleaving of new vs. review is a configurable scheduler option, not fixed). This supports recommending an explicit interleave policy in the course's deck schema.
claims.quote: New/Review Order: Whether new cards should be mixed in with review cards, shown before them, or shown after them.

claims.claim: In Common Cartridge, the 'dependency' element expresses only file/resource-asset dependencies (e.g., a resource needing shared web content or an image icon), NOT pedagogical prerequisites or unlock relationships between lessons. It is tightly restricted by resource type and cannot even be used on Discussion Topics, Web Links, Assessments, or Question Banks.
claims.quote: The 'dependency' element must only be used for resources of a particular type and for certain relationships between resource types... A Web Link - must not have a dependency; A Discussion Topic - may have a dependency on Web Content and/or Associated Content; ... Web Content - may have a dependency on Web Content; ... LTI - may have a dependency solely for an image icon. ... From this list we can see that Discussion Topic, Web Link, Assessment and Question Bank resources MUST NOT be identified by a dependency.

claims.claim: A Common Cartridge defines at most ONE organization, and that organization is a strict 'rooted-hierarchy' tree of nested <item> elements (a navigation/content tree), not a general prerequisite DAG; multiple organizations are forbidden.
claims.quote: A Common Cartridge may define a single organization, or include no organization. Multiple organizations are not permitted and the default attribute for organizations is not therefore supported. The single organization is used on import to integrate with the learning context, and defines the basic navigation structure for the package. The organization assumes the predefined "rooted-hierarchy" structure.

claims.claim: The course content tree is represented as <item> elements nested inside an <organization>, where leaf items reference resources via an identifierref attribute and intermediate items act as folders/modules (e.g., a top-level course item containing module-level items containing lesson items).
claims.quote: <organizations> <organization identifier="O_1" structure="rooted-hierarchy"> <item identifier="I_1"> <item identifier="I_00000"> <title>Psychology, Research, and You</title> <item identifier="I_00001" identifierref="I_00001_R"> <title>Learning Objectives</title> </item> <item identifier="I_00002"> <title>Study Guide</title>

claims.claim: The Common Cartridge manifest (imsmanifest.xml) cleanly separates the navigation/sequencing tree (organizations) from the content payload (resources): only learner-experience resources appear in the organization hierarchy, while operational/metadata and supplemental resources are kept out of it. This validates a manifest design that splits an index/navigation layer from a resource layer.
claims.quote: Only "Learner Experience" resources may be included in the <organization> hierarchy. Operational data (authorization, cartridge-level metadata) are defined via discrete resource types within the package. Supplemental resources must not appear in the organization hierarchy.

claims.claim: Common Cartridge v1.3 has no native conditional-release, availability, or pedagogical-sequencing mechanism (it is a deliberately constrained interoperability profile that omits IMS Simple Sequencing); the only 'conditional' constructs are QTI answer-scoring response conditions, which are unrelated to lesson unlocking.
claims.quote: The intent of this section is to provide a high-level description of a Common Cartridge... Conceptually, a Common Cartridge is a package of content and metadata that is integrated into an LMS learning context. ... The <conditionvar> depends on the question type, but the structure is always the same

claims.claim: The three Japanese moraic phonemes that have no equivalent in Portuguese — /R/ (long vowels), /Q/ (geminate consonant = sokuon っ), and /N/ (moraic nasal ん) — are the errors that persist in learners past the initial stage and are classified as 'grave' because their minimal-pair contrasts with the simple (non-long) counterparts are numerous. For pt-BR learners, mastering the mora as the basic phonological unit is presented as fundamental to overcoming them. This directly justifies giving っ, long vowels, and ん dedicated explicit treatment in kana lessons rather than glossing over them.
claims.quote: os erros que permanecem são geralmente relativos as conso[an]tes geminadas e às vogais longas, bem como, ao ensurdecimento das vogais. [...] os erros dos fonemas segmentais /R Q N/ que na hierarquização dos erros são classificados como graves, uma vez que os pares mínimos entre os fonemas acima e seus opostos simples são numerosos [...] A compreensão e o domínio da unidade básica fonológica (mora), menor que a unidade fonética (sílaba) seria fundamental para superar estes erros.

claims.claim: Vowel/long-vowel length (/R/) is empirically the single hardest contrast to PERCEIVE for beginning Brazilian learners: in the perception test it was present in 16 of the 20 words with the highest error incidence. This argues that any pt-BR kana/pronunciation design must drill long-vs-short vowel minimal pairs heavily and early (e.g., おばさん vs おばあさん).
claims.quote: Entre os três fonemas acima é /R/ que causa maiores dificuldades na percepção. Observando o quadro de erros, constatamos que este fonema esta presente em dezesseis (16) das vinte (20) palavras com maior incidência de erros.

claims.claim: Japanese /r/ (the ら-row) is a concrete distributional pitfall for Portuguese L1: Portuguese never has /r/ word-initially, and the palatalized context /rj/ (りゃ/りゅ/りょ, e.g. りゅう, りょこう) causes numerous errors where /r/ is not perceived at all; when not followed by /j/, /r/ is heard as /n/ or /l/. Japanese also lacks an /r/~/l/ opposition entirely. This supports treating the ら-row and yōon りゃ/りゅ/りょ as high-difficulty kana needing extra contrast practice for pt-BR speakers.
claims.quote: O exemplo disso e o fonema /r/ que, mesmo sendo comum aos dois sistemas, nunca ocorre em português no início do vocábulo. [...] Daí, numerosas incidências de erros nas palavras onde aparecem este fonema no citado contexto: /rjuR/ /rjokoR/, onde /r/ não é percebido. Quando [r] não vem seguido de /j/, normalmente ele é percebido como /n/ ou /l/.

claims.claim: Because /h/ is only an allophone (not a phoneme) in Portuguese, the は-row and ふ are doubly hard for pt-BR learners: they must learn both to produce /h/ and to apply its Japanese allophonic variation (e.g. ふ realized as bilabial). Documented errors include ハープ heard/produced as ハープ and ふじさん confused. This flags は/ひ/ふ/へ/ほ (and the ふ-vs-ぷ handakuten contrast) as a Portuguese-specific minimal-pair pitfall.
claims.quote: Erros como /huzisaN/ kujisan, /ha:hu/ ha:pu, resultam da dificuldade de distinguir estes sons que não pertencem ao sistema fonológico do português. [...] como /h/ não e fonema em português, mas alofone, os estudantes deverão alem de dominar a pronúncia do /h/, ainda dominar a regra de variação alofónica.

claims.claim: At the segmental level Japanese and Portuguese share the same five vowels /i e a o u/ and Japanese is overall 'less complex' segmentally than Portuguese, which favors early learnability; the residual and persistent difficulty is suprasegmental (pitch accent, duration, mora, intonation). This supports a kana-pedagogy design that front-loads the five-vowel gojūon families as low-friction wins while reserving explicit, separate treatment for length/mora/pitch.
claims.quote: São fonemas tanto em japonês como em português: /i e a o u/. [...] No aspecto segmentai, a língua japonesa mostrou ser menos complexa que a portuguesa, o que poderia favorecer a sua aprendizagem. [...] as dificuldades que persistem neles após a fase inicial da aprendizagem estão diretamente relacionadas aos fatos supra-segmentais de acento e duração.

claims.claim: LRMI's foundational learning-objective model defines three distinct competency relationships a learning resource can have — it Requires a competency (prerequisite), Teaches a competency, and Assesses a competency — which directly maps to the 'needs' (prerequisite) vs 'unlocks/teaches' distinction needed for a lesson dependency graph.
claims.quote: Key Property Areas • Learning Objectives – Requires a Competency – Teaches a Competency – Assesses a Competency

claims.claim: LRMI models prerequisite/dependency structure indirectly through competency alignment rather than direct lesson-to-lesson links: learning resources are aligned to competencies (objectives or achievement standards), and a resource that 'Requires' competency X (which another resource 'Teaches') establishes the dependency edge. This supports designing a lesson's 'needs' as a list of competency/objective IDs rather than raw lesson IDs.
claims.quote: Aligning Content to Competencies ... establishment of achievement standards to which learning activities and assessments can be aligned.

claims.claim: LRMI defines an 'Intended Use' taxonomy distinguishing the role/type of a resource, enumerating categories such as Exposition, Interactive, Lesson Plan, and Assessment, and distinguishing a source document from a learning activity — providing a precedent for a controlled enum of learningResourceType / interactivity in a courseware schema.
claims.quote: Intended Use – Source Document vs. Learning Activity – Exposition, Interactive, Lesson Plan, etc. – Assessment

claims.claim: LRMI defines an 'Audience' property area capturing intended grade or age level plus accessibility, establishing that educational level/audience is a first-class metadata property of a learning resource (mapped later to schema.org's typicalAgeRange/educationalAlignment), not a structural attribute.
claims.quote: Audience – Intended Grade or Age – Accessibility

claims.claim: LRMI deliberately chose NOT to create a single new 'learning resource' item type, instead defining learning-related properties that attach to existing/root content types (e.g. CreativeWork) — a design precedent for layering unlock/prerequisite metadata onto generic content records rather than a closed lesson type hierarchy.
claims.quote: As nearly any content could be a learning resource, we can't simply add a new item type to the Schema.org hierarchy. ... Convince Schema.org to add learning-resource properties to key root types (e.g. CreativeWork)

publishDate: 2011-11 (LRMI kickoff workshop; timeline cites Nov/Dec 2011 draft spec, March 2012 final delivery)

claims.claim: WaniKani represents prerequisite dependencies between subjects via component/amalgamation ID arrays: a kanji subject's component_subject_ids lists the radicals that must have passed assignments before that kanji unlocks, and a radical's amalgamation_subject_ids lists the kanji it composes. This is a concrete, directional dependency-graph encoding the Yomineko unlock DAG can mirror.
claims.quote: component_subject_ids Array of integers An array of numeric identifiers for the radicals that make up this kanji. Note that these are the subjects that must have passed assignments in order to unlock this subject's assignment.

claims.claim: WaniKani's unlock condition is a precise, encodable rule: a subject is unlocked into lessons only when (a) all its component subjects have reached SRS stage 5 ('passed') and (b) the user's level is at or above the subject's level. This shows unlocking is gated by both mastery (SRS state) and linear-level position, not merely lesson completion.
claims.quote: unlocked_at null or Date The timestamp when the related subject has its prerequisites satisfied and is made available in lessons. Prerequisites are: The subject components have reached SRS stage 5 once (they have been “passed”). The user's level is equal to or greater than the level of the assignment’s subject.

claims.claim: WaniKani's Assignment object is the join between a subject and the SRS scheduler, carrying distinct lifecycle timestamps the Yomineko schedule can model directly: started_at = lesson completed, available_at = next review due, passed_at = first reached SRS stage 5, burned_at = first reached SRS stage 9. The lesson step (started_at) is explicitly the moment a card enters the review cycle.
claims.quote: started_at null or Date Timestamp when the user completes the lesson for the related subject. ... available_at null or Date Timestamp when the related subject will be available in the user's review queue. ... passed_at null or Date Timestamp when the user reaches SRS stage 5 for the first time. burned_at null or Date Timestamp when the user reaches SRS stage 9 the first time.

claims.claim: WaniKani level advancement is a quantitative gate, not just completing all lessons: the next level unlocks when the user reaches a 90% passing rate on the current level's kanji assignments (kanji whose data.passed is true). This supplies a citable threshold model for tying linear topic/level progression to mastery rather than mere completion.
claims.quote: A level progression is created when a user has met the prerequisites for leveling up, which are: Reach a 90% passing rate on assignments for a user's current level with a subject_type of kanji . Passed assignments have data.passed equal to true and a data.passed_at that's in the past.

claims.claim: WaniKani's SRS is a data-driven stage table with explicit position/interval/interval_unit fields and named anchor positions (starting=1, passing=5, burning=9), where intervals are stored per stage (e.g. position 1 = 14400s/4h, position 5 = 601200s/~7d) and the _position anchors map onto the assignment timestamps. This demonstrates encoding SRS as configurable per-system stage data rather than hardcoded intervals.
claims.quote: "starting_stage_position" : 1 , "passing_stage_position" : 5 , "burning_stage_position" : 9 , "stages" : [ { "interval" : null , "position" : 0 , "interval_unit" : null }, { "interval" : 14400 , "position" : 1 , "interval_unit" : "seconds" } ... The _position fields align with the timestamps on assignment : unlocking_stage_position => unlocked_at , passing_stage_position => passed_at , etc.
